name: Triglycerides (TG)
summary: Triglycerides are the main type of fat (lipid) circulating in the blood, carried largely in VLDL and chylomicron particles and used as an energy source. They are reported on a standard lipid panel.
whatItMeans: A higher triglyceride value is associated with greater cardiovascular risk and is a hallmark of insulin resistance and metabolic syndrome, especially when paired with low HDL. Very high levels (≥ 500 mg/dL) also raise the risk of pancreatitis. Triglycerides are highly responsive to recent food intake, alcohol, and refined carbohydrates, so values can swing substantially day to day.
raises: Non-fasting state / recent high-fat or high-carbohydrate meal
Some medications (estrogens, corticosteroids, certain diuretics, retinoids)
lowers: Weight loss and reduced refined-carbohydrate intake
confounders: Non-fasting samples raise triglycerides substantially — a 9–12 hour fast is usually recommended
Alcohol intake in the days before testing inflates values
Recent acute illness or uncontrolled diabetes can transiently elevate levels

ranges.note: Normal < 150 mg/dL; 150–199 borderline high; 200–499 high; ≥ 500 very high (NCEP ATP III). Measure fasting for best accuracy.

references.title: Cholesterol testing and results: MedlinePlus Medical Encyclopedia
Mayo Clinic Q and A: Cholesterol — know your numbers
